function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Using FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and operating income, calculate the implied EBITDA-to-operating-income gap. Why is satellite depreciation economically recurring even though excluded from Adjusted EBITDA?

Let me start by parsing the filing.

Based on the provided MD&A excerpt, here is the explanation of satellite depreciation and why it is excluded from Adjusted EBITDA:

### What is Satellite Depreciation?
Satellite depreciation is the accounting process of allocating the capitalized costs of satellites over their estimated useful lives. According to the text, these capitalized costs include:
*   The costs to build the satellites (parts, labor, and allocated overhead).
*   Capitalized launch costs incurred by the Space segment to get the satellites into orbit. 

To determine the useful life and depreciation rate for these satellite assets, the company evaluates several specific factors, including:
*   On-orbit performance
*   Orbit-raise timing
*   Expected service capability
*   The evolution of constellation density and technology

### Why is it Excluded from Adjusted EBITDA?
Satellite depreciation is excluded from Adjusted EBITDA (and Segment Adjusted EBITDA) for the following reasons:
1.  **Explicit Definition:** The company strictly defines Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA as metrics that exclude **"depreciation and amortization"** (along with share-based compensation, restructuring charges, impairments, and certain other financial/tax items). 
2.  **Focus on Ongoing Operating Performance:** Management excludes depreciation because they believe it is an item that does **"not directly reflect our ongoing operating performance."** 
3.  **Cross-Period Comparability:** By removing the impact of non-cash depreciation expenses, management believes these non-GAAP measures provide "useful supplemental information" that gives investors a more "meaningful view of our financial condition and results of operations across periods."

arguments.prompt: What is the useful life of Starlink satellites and satellite replacement or replenishment cycle in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the details regarding the useful life of Starlink satellites and their replacement/replenishment cycle:

### **Estimated Useful Life**
*   **General Estimate:** Starlink satellites have an estimated useful life of **3 to 5 years**. 
*   **Specific Breakdowns:** 
    *   **Broadband satellites:** Estimated at a **5-year** useful life.
    *   **First-generation mobile satellites:** Estimated at a **3-year** useful life.
*   **Determining Factors:** These estimates are based on engineering studies, historical on-orbit performance, propellant life, utilization patterns, design enhancements across generations, and planned transitions to newer satellite technology.
*   **IT Infrastructure Mismatch:** The useful life of the satellites is inherently shorter than the information technology systems and infrastructure they host, which truncates the effective lifespan of those underlying IT systems.

### **Early Deorbiting and Safety Protocols**
*   **Proactive Deorbiting:** SpaceX often deorbits satellites *before* the end of their estimated useful lives. 
*   **Safety Rationale:** This is done primarily to reduce degradation risks that could impair the satellite's autonomous collision avoidance system and compromise overall constellation safety. (The text notes that to date, the autonomous collision avoidance system has not experienced any failures resulting in satellite loss).
*   **Space Weather Risks:** Geomagnetic storms, solar flares, and radiation can also disrupt propulsion and power systems, potentially leading to permanent damage, accelerated depreciation, or the need for early decommissioning and replacement.

### **Satellite Replacement and Replenishment Cycle**
*   **Periodic Replacement:** Because of the relatively short lifespan and proactive deorbiting protocols, the company must periodically and continuously launch replacement satellites as existing ones reach the end of their lives or are decommissioned.
*   **High-Volume Launch Cadence:** The replenishment cycle is highly active; for example, in 2025 alone, the company launched approximately **3,100 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites** to increase network capacity, improve service consistency, and replace older units.
*   **Continuous Generational Upgrades:** The replenishment cycle is heavily tied to rapid technological iteration. The company continuously replaces older models with higher-throughput successors. For instance, the constellation is currently majority composed of V2 Mini satellites, with plans to commence deploying next-generation **V3 satellites** (which offer 1 Tbps of downlink capacity) via Starship in the second half of 2026.
*   **Orbital AI Compute Lifecycle:** For future orbital AI compute satellites, the replenishment cycle will involve shifting older hardware to lower-intensity workloads as performance characteristics evolve. Once no longer needed, they will undergo controlled end-of-life disposition (such as moving to graveyard orbits), which may also occur sooner than their estimated useful lives as more advanced AI hardware becomes available.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the data I need. Let me calculate the EBITDA-to-operating-income gap and provide the analysis.

For FY2025 Connectivity Segment:
- Adjusted EBITDA: $7,168 million
- Operating Income: $4,423 million
- Gap: $7,168 - $4,423 = $2,745 million

From the reconciliation table, the adjustments from operating income to Adjusted EBITDA for Connectivity FY2025 are:
- Depreciation and amortization: $2,376 million
- Share-based compensation: $369 million
- Restructuring charges: $0
- Impairment: $0
- Total adjustments: $2,376 + $369 = $2,745 million

Check: $4,423 + $2,745 = $7,168 ✓

Now for the satellite depreciation question:
- Satellite useful life is 3-5 years (broadband: 5 years, first-gen mobile: 3 years)
- In 2025, approximately 3,100 Starlink satellites were launched
- Connectivity segment CapEx was $4,178 million in FY2025
- D&A for Connectivity was $2,376 million in FY2025

The key argument for why satellite depreciation is economically recurring:
1. Satellites have short useful lives (3-5 years)
2. They must be continuously replaced to maintain the constellation
3. The company launched ~3,100 satellites in 2025 alone for replacement and expansion
4. This creates a perpetual CapEx cycle that is operationally necessary
5. Unlike depreciation of a factory that might last 30-40 years, satellite depreciation represents a continuous, mandatory reinvestment cycle
6. The depreciation expense is essentially the accounting recognition of a real, recurring economic cost - the need to constantly build and launch replacement satellites
7. CapEx ($4,178M) actually exceeds D&A ($2,376M), showing the company is spending more on replacements/growth than the accounting depreciation recognizes

Let me calculate the gap and present the analysis.
Now let me also calculate the ratio and percentage to provide a complete analysis.

Now I have all the data I need. Let me compile the final answer.

Key findings:
1. FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $7,168M
2. FY2025 Connectivity Segment Operating Income: $4,423M
3. Gap: $2,745M (38.3% of Adjusted EBITDA)
4. The gap is composed of:
   - D&A: $2,376M (86.6% of the gap)
   - SBC: $369M (13.4% of the gap)
5. Satellite depreciation is economically recurring because:
   - Satellites have 3-5 year useful lives
   - ~3,100 satellites launched in 2025 for replacement/expansion
   - CapEx of $4,178M exceeds D&A of $2,376M (1.76x ratio)
   - Continuous mandatory reinvestment cycle

Let me submit the final result.

arguments.final_result:
## FY2025 Connectivity Segment: EBITDA-to-Operating-Income Gap Analysis

### Key Figures (from the S-1 Segment Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation)

| Metric | FY2025 ($ millions) |
|---|---|
| Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $7,168 |
| Connectivity Segment Income from Operations | $4,423 |
| **Implied Gap** | **$2,745** |

The gap of **$2,745 million** represents **38.3% of Adjusted EBITDA**, meaning more than one-third of the Connectivity segment's Adjusted EBITDA is consumed by items added back to arrive at operating income.

### Reconciliation Breakdown

| Adjustment | FY2025 ($ millions) | % of Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Depreciation & Amortization | $2,376 | 86.6% |
| Share-Based Compensation | $369 | 13.4% |
| Restructuring / Impairment | $0 | — |
| **Total Add-Backs** | **$2,745** | **100%** |

Depreciation and amortization is by far the dominant bridge item, accounting for nearly 87% of the gap.

---

### Why Satellite Depreciation Is Economically Recurring

Although SpaceX excludes depreciation and amortization from Adjusted EBITDA on the basis that it "does not directly reflect our ongoing operating performance," satellite depreciation in the Connectivity segment is **economically recurring** for the following reasons:

1. **Short Asset Lives:** Starlink broadband satellites have an estimated useful life of only **5 years**, and first-generation mobile satellites just **3 years**. This is far shorter than typical industrial or infrastructure assets (e.g., factories at 20–40 years), meaning the depreciation charge rolls over rapidly and perpetually.

2. **Mandatory Continuous Replenishment:** SpaceX launched approximately **3,100 Starlink satellites in 2025 alone** to maintain network capacity, replace deorbited units, and upgrade to newer generations. The company also proactively deorbits satellites *before* end-of-life for collision-avoidance safety, accelerating the replacement cycle.

3. **CapEx Exceeds D&A — Proof of Recurring Economic Cost:** Connectivity segment capital expenditures in FY2025 were **$4,178 million**, which is **1.76× the $2,376 million D&A charge**. This demonstrates that the cash cost of sustaining (and growing) the constellation is substantially *higher* than the non-cash depreciation add-back — the opposite of a business where depreciation is a legacy accounting artifact. The real economic reinvestment requirement is ongoing and growing.

4. **Generational Technology Treadmill:** The constellation is continuously iterating through hardware generations (currently majority V2 Mini, with V3 satellites planned for H2 2026 via Starship). Older satellites are retired not just due to physical wear but due to technological obsolescence, creating a structural, non-discretionary reinvestment cycle.

5. **No "Steady-State" Escape:** Unlike a toll road or a factory where the asset base can generate returns for decades after initial investment with minimal CapEx, the Starlink constellation requires a **perpetual CapEx annuity** just to maintain current service levels. Depreciation is the accounting shadow of this real, recurring economic obligation.

### Analytical Implication

Investors relying on Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $7,168 million as a proxy for cash earnings power should recognize that **at least $2,376 million (and likely closer to $4,178 million in actual CapEx) must be continuously reinvested** to sustain the constellation. A more economically representative earnings metric would fall somewhere between operating income ($4,423 million) and Adjusted EBITDA, depending on the maintenance-vs-growth CapEx split — which the filing does not separately disclose.

---

**Sources:**
{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## FY2025 Connectivity Segment: EBITDA-to-Operating-Income Gap Analysis

### Key Figures (from the S-1 Segment Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation)

| Metric | FY2025 ($ millions) |
|---|---|
| Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $7,168 |
| Connectivity Segment Income from Operations | $4,423 |
| **Implied Gap** | **$2,745** |

The gap of **$2,745 million** represents **38.3% of Adjusted EBITDA**, meaning more than one-third of the Connectivity segment's Adjusted EBITDA is consumed by items added back to arrive at operating income.

### Reconciliation Breakdown

| Adjustment | FY2025 ($ millions) | % of Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Depreciation & Amortization | $2,376 | 86.6% |
| Share-Based Compensation | $369 | 13.4% |
| Restructuring / Impairment | $0 | — |
| **Total Add-Backs** | **$2,745** | **100%** |

Depreciation and amortization is by far the dominant bridge item, accounting for nearly 87% of the gap.

---

### Why Satellite Depreciation Is Economically Recurring

Although SpaceX excludes depreciation and amortization from Adjusted EBITDA on the basis that it "does not directly reflect our ongoing operating performance," satellite depreciation in the Connectivity segment is **economically recurring** for the following reasons:

1. **Short Asset Lives:** Starlink broadband satellites have an estimated useful life of only **5 years**, and first-generation mobile satellites just **3 years**. This is far shorter than typical industrial or infrastructure assets (e.g., factories at 20–40 years), meaning the depreciation charge rolls over rapidly and perpetually.

2. **Mandatory Continuous Replenishment:** SpaceX launched approximately **3,100 Starlink satellites in 2025 alone** to maintain network capacity, replace deorbited units, and upgrade to newer generations. The company also proactively deorbits satellites *before* end-of-life for collision-avoidance safety, accelerating the replacement cycle.

3. **CapEx Exceeds D&A — Proof of Recurring Economic Cost:** Connectivity segment capital expenditures in FY2025 were **$4,178 million**, which is **1.76× the $2,376 million D&A charge**. This demonstrates that the cash cost of sustaining (and growing) the constellation is substantially *higher* than the non-cash depreciation add-back — the opposite of a business where depreciation is a legacy accounting artifact. The real economic reinvestment requirement is ongoing and growing.

4. **Generational Technology Treadmill:** The constellation is continuously iterating through hardware generations (currently majority V2 Mini, with V3 satellites planned for H2 2026 via Starship). Older satellites are retired not just due to physical wear but due to technological obsolescence, creating a structural, non-discretionary reinvestment cycle.

5. **No "Steady-State" Escape:** Unlike a toll road or a factory where the asset base can generate returns for decades after initial investment with minimal CapEx, the Starlink constellation requires a **perpetual CapEx annuity** just to maintain current service levels. Depreciation is the accounting shadow of this real, recurring economic obligation.

### Analytical Implication

Investors relying on Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $7,168 million as a proxy for cash earnings power should recognize that **at least $2,376 million (and likely closer to $4,178 million in actual CapEx) must be continuously reinvested** to sustain the constellation. A more economically representative earnings metric would fall somewhere between operating income ($4,423 million) and Adjusted EBITDA, depending on the maintenance-vs-growth CapEx split — which the filing does not separately disclose.
